FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data at baseline (Y0; ages 9-10) and two-year follow-up (Y2; ages 11-12)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/social media variables: addictive screen use (ASU) composite, addictive videogaming, addictive social media use, and addictive phone use. Abstract states 'addictive videogaming, addictive social media use, addictive phone use, and a composite measure of ASU'

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multiple inferential statistical tests: mixed-effects regressions linking ASU/addictive videogaming/addictive phone use to cortical functional connectomic maturation (AFC) and nucleus accumbens (NAc) reward activation. E.g., 'βM1 = -.056, CI = [-.083, -.027], p < .001' for ASU vs AFC

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: β coefficients with CIs and p-values throughout. E.g., 'cross-domains addictive screen use at Y2 (N = 5683; βM1 = -.056, CI = [-.083, -.027], p < .001)'; 'addictive videogaming at Y2 (N = 4522; βM1 = -.060, CI = [-.087, -.031], p < .001)'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis. Longitudinal design with baseline neuroimaging predicting Y2 ASU outcomes. Mixed-effects regressions and mediation analyses conducted

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging, 2026. Not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply. While ASU is the dependent variable, the paper includes multiple individual psychological/neural predictors (cortical connectomic maturation, nucleus accumbens anticipatory reward activation) beyond demographic factors. Per criterion (b), FT_EC7 only applies when predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual; here they are neurodevelopmental. Additionally, paper reports indirect effects and mediation pathways involving cognitive task performance
notes: E16 applies: Individual-characteristic predictors (cortical maturation, reward processing) of phone/SM outcome; MET regardless of direction/timeframe

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data. Abstract states 'Participants were 6,467 youth (39.2% female) who reported playing digital games, from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study in the U.S.' Data from ages 12-13

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures problematic gaming (video games) as primary exposure variable, NOT smartphone or social media specifically. Abstract: 'problematic gaming' as the exposure; outcome is psychotic-like experiences (PLEs). No separately measured phone/SM variable. E17 applies: video games with unspecified platform (or console/PC games in general context) without distinct phone/SM measurement = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: N/A - fails FT_IC2 screening. Paper examines association of problematic gaming (not a qualifying phone/SM variable per FT_IC2) with PLEs

FT_EC7_reason: N/A - paper excluded at FT_IC2. Problematic gaming (video games) does not meet smartphone/social media exposure requirement
notes: E17 applies: Video games unspecified platform (console or PC gaming not specified as phone app) → NOT_MET for FT_IC2. Paper excluded at FT_IC2 threshold per coder prompt criteria

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data. Methods: 'We included 2294 participants from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (Mage = 10.03, 50.6% female) who had resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data at baseline and PUSM scores at the four-year follow-up'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures problematic use of social media (PUSM) specifically using Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ). Methods: 'the 6-item Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ)...was used to measure salience, tolerance, mood modification, relapse, withdrawal, and conflict associated with PUSM'. This is a specific, validated social media problematic use scale

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests linking PUSM to neural outcome. Connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM) with correlation analysis: 'CPM successfully predicted PUSM severity (combined positive and negative networks: r = 0.055, p = 0.016)' with permutation testing 'p = 0.02'. Machine-learning coefficients count per E4

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: Pearson's r correlations and p-values. Results section: 'r = 0.066, p = 0.003' (without covariates); 'r = 0.055, p = 0.016' (with covariates); permutation testing 'p = 0.02'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis from ABCD. Methods describe longitudinal design with baseline rs-fMRI (ages 9-10) predicting PUSM at four-year follow-up (ages 13-14)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in NeuroImage 329 (2026) 121829. Peer-reviewed article, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply. Although PUSM is the dependent variable in analyses, the paper includes individual psychological/neural predictors (brain functional connectivity patterns, default mode network, sensorimotor network, cognitive control and self-regulation systems) well beyond demographic/contextual factors. Per criterion (b), FT_EC7 only applies when predictors are ONLY demographic (SES, race, sex, age, etc.); the predictors here are neural/neurodevelopmental, not demographic alone
notes: E4 applies: Machine-learning approach (CPM) with reported correlation coefficients → MET for FT_IC4. Paper uses whole-brain functional connectivity to predict PUSM severity; neural features are individual-characteristic predictors per E16 logic
